content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)'. Selection reform is now live government policy with EU tailwind but faces low-probability cross-party durability, high reversal risk on government turnover, and non-negotiable stakeholder preconditions (workforce guarantees, statutory funding) that the minister has not yet addressed, making it politically feasible in the medium term (2027–2028) only if the government narrows its scope to pilot-phase rebalancing coupled with explicit quality-levelling commitments, abandons abolition rhetoric, and negotiates binding funding guarantees—the opposite of which suggests either stalled-indefinite consultation or legislative reversal within one electoral cycle.
opportunities: Shrinks the selective track's footprint without requiring statutory redefinition of the secondary-education age boundary, potentially avoiding the felmenő-rendszer and ~1-year lead-time obligations that a structural reform would trigger.

Aligns policy with the demographic reality that the exam's original rationing function is eroding, as evidenced by falling 8-year-track applications (-14% year-on-year) alongside flat overall enrolment.
strengths: The Tisza Party's 12 April 2026 electoral victory (141 of 199 seats, ~53% party-list) created a constitutional two-thirds mandate; education received a separate ministry for the first time since 2010, signalling structural reform is now institutionally feasible.

Education Minister Lannert Judit (June 2026) publicly stated that 6/8-year gimnázium students do not develop faster than age-cohort peers; the system's only function is elite self-selection and socioeconomic status reproduction, not pedagogy. She framed the question as a societal-policy choice, not technical, and explicitly committed to review rather than abolition, with current gimnáziums to retain their position.
threats: Reversal requires legislative repeal since score thresholds are maintainer-level administrative practice rather than statute, offering more procedural friction than S1's decree-based mechanism, but a parliamentary majority change during the multi-year rollout still poses a significant reversal risk given the Fidesz framing of any selection-related change and the government's own pattern of rapidly reversing predecessor reforms.

Church and foundation maintainers, protected by Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior operating conditions, may simply expand to absorb displaced demand, neutralizing the intended contraction and creating a policy-credibility problem.
weaknesses: Will the government publish a formal legislative timetable, Oktatási Hivatal review mandate, or government resolution on selection reform, moving it beyond consultative intent?

Are statutory (vs annual-budget) funding-floor guarantees politically achievable under the projected fiscal consolidation pressures, or will compensatory funding remain contingent on annual budget cycles?
